title: Using 2D Diffusion Models to Guide 3D
concept: Rather than training on scarce 3D data, some methods use a pretrained 2D image diffusion model to judge whether rendered views of a 3D object look realistic.

exercises.prompt: If you wanted to check whether a sculpture looks realistic from every angle, what could you do?

options.text: Take photos of it from many angles and see if each photo looks like a real object
Only look at it from the front once

exercises.explain: Checking many photo angles is exactly the trick AI uses to judge if a 3D shape looks real!

cards.title: No 3D Teacher? No Problem!
cards.body: There isn't a lot of 3D data to train AI on — but there are BILLIONS of 2D images online. What if an AI that's a genius at judging 2D pictures could teach a 3D model to look good, just by checking its photos?

cards.title: 2D-Guided 3D Generation
cards.body: This is a technique where a 3D object is built (or refined) by rendering it as 2D images from different angles, then asking a pretrained 2D image diffusion model 'does this look real?' The 2D model's feedback nudges the 3D shape to become more realistic, even though the 2D model never saw 3D data.

cards.title: The Photo Critic
cards.body: Imagine a sculptor who can't see in 3D, but has a friend who's amazing at judging photographs. The sculptor keeps snapping pictures of their clay model from different sides and shows them to the friend. The friend says 'that angle looks weird' or 'that looks great!' Slowly, using only photo feedback, the sculptor perfects a full 3D statue.

cards.title: Building a 3D Apple
cards.body: An AI starts with a rough blob. It renders the blob as a 2D image from the front — a pretrained 2D diffusion model (trained on millions of real photos) checks it and says 'not round enough, not red enough.' The AI adjusts the 3D shape, renders it again from a new angle, gets more feedback, and repeats until every rendered view looks like a real apple.

cards.title: Myth: You need a 3D dataset to make 3D AI
cards.body: Not always true! Since real 3D data is rare and expensive to collect, clever methods reuse powerful 2D image models (trained on huge photo datasets) as 'judges.' The 3D model learns indirectly, guided entirely through 2D snapshots — no 3D training data required.

cards.title: Two Ways to Learn 3D
cards.body: One approach needs real 3D examples to copy from. The other borrows a 2D expert's eye and checks renders from many angles instead.

left.text: Needs lots of real 3D models to learn from — but 3D data is rare and hard to collect

right.text: Uses a pretrained 2D image model to judge rendered views — leverages tons of existing 2D photos instead

exercises.prompt: Why do some methods use a pretrained 2D diffusion model to guide 3D generation?

options.text: Because 3D data is scarce, but 2D image data is abundant
Because 2D models are faster at rendering colors
Because 3D shapes are easier to draw than 2D images
Because 2D models can directly output 3D meshes

exercises.explain: 3D training data is limited, but there's a massive amount of 2D image data available, so reusing a 2D model's judgment is a clever workaround.

exercises.prompt: In this method, the 2D diffusion model is trained directly on 3D shapes before being used to guide 3D generation.
exercises.explain: False! The 2D diffusion model is pretrained on 2D images only. It never sees 3D data directly — it just judges rendered 2D views.

exercises.sentence: To guide a 3D model, the AI renders it as ___ images from different angles and checks them with a 2D diffusion model.
exercises.explain: The 3D object gets rendered into flat 2D images (like photos), which the 2D diffusion model can then judge.

exercises.prompt: What does the pretrained 2D diffusion model actually check to help guide the 3D shape?

options.text: Whether rendered 2D views of the 3D object look realistic
Whether the 3D object's file size is small enough
Whether the 3D object has enough polygons

exercises.explain: The 2D model looks at rendered snapshots and judges realism, giving feedback that shapes the 3D model — just like a photo critic guiding a sculptor.

exercises.prompt: An AI team wants to build a system that creates realistic 3D chairs, but they only have 200 real 3D chair models — not nearly enough to train a good 3D generator. However, they have access to a powerful 2D diffusion model trained on millions of furniture photos. What's the smartest approach?

options.text: Give up on 3D generation since there isn't enough 3D data
Use the 2D diffusion model to judge rendered views of the 3D chair from many angles, guiding it to look realistic
Train the 2D diffusion model on the 200 3D chairs instead
Only ever look at the chair from one fixed angle during training

exercises.explain: Correct! By rendering the 3D chair from multiple angles and letting the powerful 2D model judge each view, the team can guide realistic 3D generation without needing tons of 3D training data.
